prs.title: fix: garbage-collect API key Secret by setting an ownerReference
prs.body:
## Description
- Create the `APIKey` first, then create the `Secret` with an `ownerReference` to it, so Kubernetes garbage-collects the Secret automatically when the APIKey is deleted.
- Roll back the APIKey if Secret creation fails, so a partial failure doesn't leave a key without its Secret.

Reordering is safe: the APIKey references the Secret by name (`secretRef.name`), which is known up front.

Fixes #512

prs.title: fix: clean up elicitation idmap entries on interrupted ext_proc streams
prs.body:
While reading through the elicitation flow I noticed the cleanup contract in `docs/design/notifications.md:339`:

> Mappings persist for the tool call duration and are removed when the response stream ends. In Redis-backed deployments, a 1-hour TTL acts as a safety net for orphaned entries.

The "removed when the response stream ends" half doesn't hold for all the ways a stream can end.

In `internal/mcp-router/server.go:Process()`, `rewriter.Flush(ctx)` is called from exactly one site — line 285, inside `ProcessingRequest_ResponseBody` when `endOfStream` is true. After the rewriter is created at line 253 there are several `return err` paths that bypass it:

- `stream.Recv()` error on the next iteration (client disconnect / Envoy stream close)
- `stream.Send` error on the routing response
- `stream.Send` error on the response body

Each abandoned stream then leaks N entries in `ElicitationMap`, where N is the number of `elicitation/create` messages already buffered before the stream died.

For the in-memory `idmap` (`internal/idmap/inmemory.go`) this is unbounded — unlike `redis.go` it has no `entryTTL`, no safety net. Memory grows with streaming-tool-call traffic that includes elicitations. The Redis-backed deployments still hold the orphaned entries until the 1-hour TTL kicks in.

The fix is two small pieces: a function-level `defer` in `Process` that calls `rewriter.Flush` on any exit path, plus making `Flush` idempotent (clear `w.gatewayIDs` after the Remove loop) so the happy-path call followed by the defer is a clean no-op.

Added a subtest under `TestSSERewriter_Flush` covering the idempotency contract. All `internal/` tests pass with `-race`.

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Improved resource cleanup during stream processing errors. The system now properly handles orphaned entries when stream receive/send errors occur, ensuring cleanup happens regardless of exit conditions. Made resource cleanup operations idempotent to prevent duplicate processing attempts.

prs.title: Fix Redis getOrSet cache stampede with distributed lock

labels.name: backend
labels.description: Evaluation subsystem (judge)

labels.name: php
labels.description: Pull requests that update Php code

labels.name: size/L
labels.description: Large PR (≤200 lines)

prs.body:
## Problem

When `OMEGAUP_CACHE_IMPLEMENTATION` is `redis`, `RedisCacheAdapter::getOrSet()` does not take a lock before calling the expensive `$setFunc()` on a cache miss. Every concurrent request that sees a miss runs `$setFunc()` and then races to set the key; only one write wins, but all have already run the callback. That causes a cache stampede (N redundant recomputations) and can spike load and latency when the cache is cold or after invalidation.

The base `CacheAdapter::getOrSet()` (used by APCu/InProcess) avoids this with a file lock per `lockGroup`. The Redis adapter overrides `getOrSet()` and did not use any lock (and file locks don’t work across multiple frontend servers). The TODO at lines 336–338 in `Cache.php` noted this and suggested Redlock.


## Solution

- **Acquire a Redis lock** per `lockGroup` before calling `$setFunc()`: `SET lockKey token NX PX 30000` (30s TTL).
- **Waiters** that fail to acquire the lock poll `fetch($key)` until the key is set (or the lock expires), then return from cache.
- **Release** the lock only if we still hold it using a Lua script that deletes the key only when its value equals our token.
- **Double-check** after acquiring the lock: if the key was already set (e.g. by a previous holder), return it without running `$setFunc()`.

Behavior now matches the base adapter: only one process runs `$setFunc()` per key/lockGroup on a miss; others wait and then read from cache (single flight, no stampede).

Fixes: #8962

prs.title: Enforce POST for mutating API endpoints (reject GET with 405)

labels.name: backend
labels.description: Evaluation subsystem (judge)

labels.name: php
labels.description: Pull requests that update Php code

labels.name: python
labels.description: Pull requests that update Python code

labels.name: size/XL
labels.description: Extra large PR (>200 lines)

prs.body:
## Problem
State-changing API endpoints (e.g. `User::apiUpdate`) could be called via GET query parameters because the server used `$_REQUEST` and did not check HTTP method.

## Solution
- Reject GET for mutating endpoints and return **405 Method Not Allowed** with a clear error message.
- Read-only endpoints (list, details, profile, etc.) still allow GET.
- Two read-only endpoints whose names contain a mutating verb (`listAssociatedIdentities`, `statusVerified`) are allowlisted so GET remains allowed for them.

Fixes: #9001

prs.title: fix: replace real-looking secrets in config.default.php with placeholder

labels.name: backend
labels.description: Evaluation subsystem (judge)

labels.name: php
labels.description: Pull requests that update Php code

labels.name: size/M
labels.description: Medium PR (≤100 lines)

prs.body:
## Description

Replaces real-looking secrets in `config.default.php` with placeholders to avoid insecure defaults. Environments that relied on these defaults could silently use public, known secrets.

## Problem

`config.default.php` contained high-entropy secrets that looked like production values:
- `OMEGAUP_GITSERVER_SECRET_KEY` / `OMEGAUP_GITSERVER_PUBLIC_KEY`
- `OMEGAUP_GRADER_SECRET`
- `OMEGAUP_COURSE_CLONE_SECRET_KEY`
- `OMEGAUP_GOOGLE_SECRET` / `OMEGAUP_GOOGLE_CLIENTID`
- `OMEGAUP_EXPERIMENT_SECRET`

Any deployment (staging, new prod region, developer env) that did not override these in `config.php` would use these public secrets, allowing:
- Gitserver auth forgery
- Grader request forgery
- Course clone token forgery

## Solution

- **config.default.php:** Replace these with `CHANGE_ME` / `xxxxx` placeholders.
- **test_config.default.php:** Add test-only secret definitions so tests keep working.
- **prepare-env action & developer-environment.sh:** Add explicit secret overrides for CI and dev environments.

Misconfigured deployments now fail when these values are used instead of silently using public secrets.

fixes : #8992

prs.title: Use CSPRNG and sha256 for password reset tokens

labels.name: backend
labels.description: Evaluation subsystem (judge)

labels.name: php
labels.description: Pull requests that update Php code

labels.name: size/XS
labels.description: Very small PR (≤10 lines)

prs.body:
### Summary

This PR strengthens the security of password-reset tokens by switching from weak randomness (`rand()` + `uniqid()` + `md5`) to a cryptographically secure random source, and by using `sha256` instead of `sha1` when storing and verifying reset digests. This addresses [#8988] (Weak randomness for password-reset tokens in `ApiUtils::getRandomString()`).


Fixes: #8988 

### Changes

- `frontend/server/src/ApiUtils.php`
  - Change `ApiUtils::getRandomString()` from:
    - `md5(uniqid(strval(rand()), true))`
    - **to** `bin2hex(random_bytes(32))` (256-bit CSPRNG, hex encoded).

- `frontend/server/src/Controllers/Reset.php`
  - In `apiCreate()` and `apiGenerateToken()`:
    - Store the reset digest using `hash('sha256', $token)` instead of `hash('sha1', $token)`.
  - In `apiUpdate()`:
    - Verify the provided `reset_token` by comparing `hash('sha256', $resetToken)` with the stored `reset_digest`.

prs.title: [BUG] Nil pointer dereference in local proxy (localDelete/localPost)
prs.body:
#### What type of PR is this?
bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:
Adds nil checks for RequestInfo in local proxy handlers to prevent panics when RequestInfo is not present in the request context.

The code previously ignored the `ok` return value from `apirequest.RequestInfoFrom()`, which could cause nil pointer dereferences in:
- `localDelete` - when building Status response (info.Name, info.Namespace, info.Resource)
- `localPost` - when checking info.Resource for events handling
- `localReqCache` - when building NotFound error (reqInfo.APIGroup, reqInfo.Resource, reqInfo.Name)
- `WithFakeTokenInject` (faketoken.go) - when checking info.Resource for token requests

Fix unchecked multiplication in `List` branch that could also overflow

Fixes #942 

On 32-bit systems, `usize::MAX = 4,294,967,295`. With realistic quantum ML inputs:
- 20 qubits → `current_size = 2^20 = 1,048,576`
- With `batch.num_rows() = 4,096`: `1,048,576 × 4,096 = 4,294,967,296` → overflows

Using `.expect()` causes a panic, which crashes the program. The fix returns a `Result` error that callers can handle gracefully.

This follows the same pattern used in `tensorflow.rs` (lines 84–89).

prs.title: fix: race condition in cache manager's inMemoryCache
prs.body:
## Description

Fixed critical race condition in cache manager's `inMemoryCache` map that could lead to data corruption, panics, and inconsistent cache state.

## Problem

The `inMemoryCache` map was accessed concurrently without proper synchronization:
1. `saveWatchObject` runs in a goroutine (via `CacheResponse`) and calls `updateInMemoryCache`
2. `queryInMemoryCache` returns pointers directly from the map after releasing the lock
3. Objects stored in cache could be modified concurrently by multiple goroutines

## Solution
- **Deep copy on write**: Objects are deep copied before storing in `inMemoryCacheFor` to ensure cached objects are independent
- **Deep copy on read**: Objects are deep copied while holding the RLock in `queryInMemoryCache` to prevent map entry replacement during copy
- **Nil safety**: Added defensive nil checks to handle edge cases gracefully

prs.title: fix: restore from backup and return error on ReplaceComponentList create/write failure
prs.body:
## Description 

`ReplaceComponentList` in `pkg/yurthub/storage/disk/storage.go` had a critical bug:

- On `CreateDir` or `CreateFile` failure it used `continue` and kept going.
- At the end it **always** ran `DeleteDir(tmpPath)`, removing the backup even when some writes had failed.
- The function could still return `nil` (success), so callers assumed the replace succeeded.

**Result:** Partial new content on disk, backup deleted → **data loss**. In autonomy mode the edge cache (pods, configmaps, etc.) could be corrupted.

prs.title: Bugfix : remove deprecated rand.Seed() calls
prs.body:
#### What this PR does / why we need it:

This PR removes deprecated `rand.Seed()` calls that will cause compilation failures in future Go versions. The `rand.New(rand.NewSource(time.Now().UnixNano()))` function already seeds the random number generator, making the explicit `Seed()` call redundant and deprecated.

**Why this is needed:**
The `rand.Seed()` function has been deprecated in Go 1.20+ and will be removed in future versions. This change prevents future compilation failures and follows Go best practices. The random number generator created by `rand.New()` is already properly seeded, so the additional `Seed()` call was unnecessary.

prs.title: fix: use correct `WebAssembly.instantiate` callback signature in `wasm/module-wrapper`

labels.name: difficulty: 2
labels.description: May require some initial design or R&D, but should be straightforward to resolve and/or implement.

prs.body:
## Description

> What is the purpose of this pull request?
Fixes a critical bug in `@stdlib/wasm/module-wrapper` where the `onResolve` callback in both `initialize()` and `initializeAsync()` methods incorrectly expected two separate parameters (`module`, `instance`) when `WebAssembly.instantiate()` returns a Promise resolving to a single object `{module, instance}`. Updates the `onResolve` callback signature to accept a single `result` parameter and correctly extract `result.module` and `result.instance` properties.Adds comprehensive test coverage for both async initialization methods to verify the fix works correctly and prevent regressions.

## Related Issues

> Does this pull request have any related issues?

This pull request has no related issues.

### Bug Impact

The bug caused `this._instance` to be `undefined` after async initialization, breaking all WebAssembly operations that depend on instance exports. Any code using `initialize()` or `initializeAsync()` would fail when trying to access `this._instance.exports.*`.

### Technical Details

The `WebAssembly.instantiate()` API returns `Promise<{module: WebAssembly.Module, instance: WebAssembly.Instance}>`. The previous implementation incorrectly destructured this as two separate parameters in the Promise `.then()` callback, which only receives a single argument.

### Testing

All new tests pass and verify:
- `_module` and `_instance` are correctly set after initialization
- Both properties are instances of `WebAssembly.Module` and `WebAssembly.Instance` respectively
- The `exports` property is accessible after initialization

### Problem
The remote client completely ignores the `--detach-keys` option for `podman exec` sessions. Users cannot detach from exec sessions using custom key sequences.

The `ExecStartAndAttach()` function in `pkg/bindings/containers/attach.go` hardcodes empty detach keys:

```go
// Line 461 - BEFORE
_, err := detach.Copy(socket, options.InputStream, []byte{})

// Line 482 - BEFORE  
_, err := detach.Copy(options.GetOutputStream(), socket, []byte{})
//                                                        ^^^^^^^^ HARDCODED!
```

This fix complements the recent fix for empty `--detach-keys=""` in PR #27425 , maintaining consistency across attach, run, start, and exec commands.

prs.body:
### Problem
The remote client (`podman --remote`) incorrectly throws an error when `--detach-keys=""` is specified:
```
Error: invalid detach keys: Unknown character: ''
```

According to the documentation (`docs/source/markdown/options/detach-keys.md`):
> Specifying "" disables this feature
The local client handles this correctly and was fixed in v1.7.0 (issue #5166), but the remote client was missed.

The remote client bindings call `term.ToBytes()` directly on the user-provided string, which fails for empty strings. The local implementation checks for empty string first before calling `term.ToBytes()`.

### Solution
Added the same empty string check in `pkg/bindings/containers/attach.go` that exists in the local code path (`libpod/oci_conmon_attach_common.go:199`):

```go
if options.GetDetachKeys() == "" {
    detachKeysInBytes = []byte{}
} else {
    detachKeysInBytes, err = term.ToBytes(options.GetDetachKeys())
    // ...
}
```

Fixes: #27414

```release-note
'None'
```

prs.title: feat: Add UID support for Kubernetes objects (LFX Mentorship T3)
prs.body:
## Add UID support for Kubernetes objects (LFX Mentorship T3)

This PR implements UID support for 3 Kubernetes objects (Pods, Nodes, Services) as required by the LFX Mentorship T3 coding challenge. I've added UID fields to the core data structures and enhanced the metrics emission to include UIDs as Prometheus labels.


 what I implemented:


- Added `UID` field to `Node` and `Service` structs in clustercache
- Updated `TransformNode` and `TransformService` functions to extract UIDs from K8s API objects
- Enhanced node metrics (`kube_node_labels`, `kube_node_status_capacity`, `kube_node_status_capacity_cpu_cores`) with UID support
- Added UID support to `service_selector_labels` metric
- Updated Prometheus queries to include UID in grouping for cost model integration


### Prometheus queries now work:

```promql
kube_pod_labels{uid="pod-uid-123"}
kube_node_labels{uid="node-uid-456"}
service_selector_labels{uid="service-uid-789"}
```

### Understanding the OpenCost pipeline:

I studied how OpenCost works and implemented UID support across the entire pipeline:
1. K8s API objects > clustercache (UID extraction)
2. clustercache > metrics emission (UID in Prometheus labels)
3. metrics > cost model queries (UID in query grouping)
4. cost model  >allocation calculations (UID available for cost tracking)

This gives us stable object identification throughout the system, which will help with cost analysis and debugging.

The implementation follows OpenCost's existing patterns and maintains backward compatibility while adding the requested UID functionality.

- For HTTP routes: always uses `RequestTimeout` (no change)
- Set to `"0s"` to disable timeout for infinite streaming

prs.title: feat: Add Tags column to CVE export CSV
prs.body:
## What this PR does / why we need it

This PR resolves the issue where CVE exports do not contain artifact tags, making it difficult for users to correlate vulnerabilities with human-readable image tags.

By adding a "Tags" column to the CSV export, this change significantly improves the usability of the vulnerability report, allowing users to quickly identify which tags are affected by a CVE without having to manually look up artifact digests.

## Which issue(s) this PR fixes

Fixes #21675

## What this PR contains

- **`src/pkg/scan/export/model.go`**: Added a `Tags` field to the `Data` struct with the proper `csv:"Tags"` annotation.
- **`src/pkg/scan/export/manager.go`**: Updated the `VulnScanReportQueryTemplate` SQL query to use `string_agg` to aggregate comma-separated tag names for each artifact.
- **Test Files**: Updated `createDataRecords` test helpers in `export_data_selector_test.go` and `scan_data_export_test.go` to include the new `Tags` field, ensuring our tests remain valid.

## How to test this PR

1.  Navigate to a project in the Harbor UI.
2.  Ensure you have an artifact with at least one vulnerability and one or more tags (e.g., `latest`, `v1.0`).
3.  Go to the project's **Vulnerabilities** tab.
4.  Click **Export CVE**.
5.  Open the downloaded CSV file.
6.  **Verification**: Confirm that a **"Tags"** column exists and contains the correct, comma-separated tag names for the artifact.

## Special notes for your reviewer

- This is a non-breaking, additive feature.
- The SQL query uses a `LEFT JOIN` to ensure artifacts without tags are still included in the report (with an empty `Tags` field).
- Handles multiple tags on a single artifact by comma-separating them, consistent with how `CWE Ids` are handled.
- All related unit tests in the `pkg/scan/export`, `controller/scandataexport`, and `jobservice/job/impl/scandataexport` packages are passing with high coverage (63-92%).

## Summary
The `javascript` job in CI does not set a Node.js version and relies on whatever the runner provides. This can cause "works on my machine" differences and break when GitHub upgrades the runner's Node.

## Problem
- **Where:** [`.github/workflows/ci.yml`](.github/workflows/ci.yml) — `javascript` job

## Proposed solution
Add a step **before** "Install yarn dependencies" in the `javascript` job:

- name: Set up Node.js

  with:
    node-version: '20'
    cache: 'yarn'

The cache is never invalidated when course or assignment data (or anything that affects progress) changes. Users can see stale progress for up to 12 hours after updates.

When course or assignment data (or progress-affecting data) is updated, all entries for that course under SCHOOL_STUDENTS_PROGRESS should be invalidated (e.g. via Cache::invalidateAllKeys(\OmegaUp\Cache::SCHOOL_STUDENTS_PROGRESS)), so the next request recomputes fresh progress. issues.body:
`config.default.php` contains high-entropy secrets that look like production values. They’re committed to the repo, so anyone can use them.

**Problem**

If a deployment relies on defaults (staging, new prod region, forgot to set `config.php`), it uses these public secrets. No error, no warning.

**Affected keys (lines 37–77):**

- `OMEGAUP_GRADER_SECRET` — can forge grader requests

issues.title: [BUG] Weak randomness for password-reset tokens in `ApiUtils::getRandomString()`
issues.body:
## Why This Is a Problem
- rand() and uniqid() are not cryptographically secure and are documented as unsuitable for security-sensitive tokens.
- md5() is a deterministic hash and does not add entropy or make weak randomness secure.


Since these tokens are used in password-reset links, an attacker who can approximate the generation time and send many guesses has a better chance of guessing a valid token than they should.
This contrasts with Session::registerSession(), which already uses random_bytes() + sha256, following modern best practices.

issues.title: [BUG]Redis getOrSet causes cache stampede when OMEGAUP_CACHE_IMPLEMENTATION is redis
issues.body:
## Current Behavior

When `OMEGAUP_CACHE_IMPLEMENTATION` is `redis`, `RedisCacheAdapter::getOrSet()` in `frontend/server/src/Cache.php` does not acquire any distributed lock before calling the expensive `$setFunc()` callback on a cache miss. Every concurrent request that sees a miss runs `$setFunc()` and then races to set the key. Only one request wins the `SET NX`, but all of them have already executed the callback. This is a cache stampede: N concurrent misses cause N redundant recomputations (e.g. N heavy DB/aggregation calls), which can spike load and latency when cache is cold or after invalidation.

The base `CacheAdapter::getOrSet()` (used by APCCacheAdapter) prevents this with a file lock per `lockGroup` (`/tmp/omegaup-cache-*.lock`). `RedisCacheAdapter` overrides `getOrSet()` and does not use that lock (and file locks don't work across multiple frontend servers). The TODO at lines 336–338 in `Cache.php` documents this: "This does not acquire a global lock to avoid this from being called multiple times. Maybe we should try a Redlock here."

`Cache::getFromCacheOrSet()` is used in many places (ranks, contest/problem lists, session, user profile, problem statement/settings, run counts, course progress, etc.), so the impact is high when Redis is used with multiple frontend processes.

## Expected Behavior

On a cache miss, only one process should run `$setFunc()` per key (or per lock group); others should wait or retry and then read the value written by the first. Behavior should match the file-lock path used by the base adapter (single flight / no stampede).

issues.title: [QDP] Add TensorFlow .pb support to unified encode() path
issues.body:
`QdpEngine.encode()` auto-detects parquet/arrow/feather/npy/pt/pth, but `.pb` (TensorFlow TensorProto) is only available via `encode_from_tensorflow()`. Users passing a `.pb` path to `encode()` get “Unsupported file format…”. Add `.pb` handling to the unified path for parity.

 Current behavior
- `engine.encode("data.pb", num_qubits, "amplitude")` raises unsupported-format error.
- Users must call `encode_from_tensorflow()` explicitly.

Expected behavior
- `encode()` recognizes `.pb` (TensorFlow TensorProto) and routes to the TensorFlow reader.

issues.title: [QDP] Support torch CUDA tensor inputs directly avoid (GPU→CPU→GPU copies)
issues.body:
QDP’s Python binding currently rejects torch CUDA tensors and only supports CPU tensors, forcing users to copy GPU→CPU→GPU (or failing). Add a CUDA-tensor path that keeps data on GPU and runs encoding without host staging.

Current behavior
- Passing a torch CUDA tensor to `QdpEngine.encode(...)` raises an error like:
  - “Only CPU tensors are currently supported for this path…”
- Users must `.cpu()` first, which adds large overhead and breaks GPU-only pipelines.

Expected behavior
- `QdpEngine.encode(torch_tensor_cuda, ...)` works for:
  - 1D tensors (single sample)
  - 2D tensors (batch)
- No GPU→CPU copy is required.
- Clear errors for unsupported cases (dtype, contiguity, device mismatch).

issues.title: [Bug] Backend config validation missing in QuMat.__init__()
issues.body:
The `QuMat.__init__()` method currently lacks validation for the `backend_config` parameter. While the docstring states that `KeyError` is raised for missing required keys, and the test suite (`testing/qumat/test_create_circuit.py`) expects specific error messages, the actual implementation does not perform any validation.

**Current Behavior:**

When invalid `backend_config` is provided, the code raises generic Python errors instead of clear, user-friendly error messages:

1. **Type validation missing**: Passing `None` or a non-dict raises `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable` instead of `ValueError`
2. **Key validation missing**: Missing `backend_name` or `backend_options` raises bare `KeyError: 'backend_name'` instead of `KeyError` with a helpful message

**Expected Behavior:**

Based on existing tests in `testing/qumat/test_create_circuit.py`, the implementation should:

1. Raise `ValueError("backend_config must be a dictionary")` when a non-dict is passed
2. Raise `KeyError` matching `"missing required key.*backend_name"` when `backend_name` is missing
3. Raise `KeyError` matching `"missing required key.*backend_options"` when `backend_options` is missing

issues.body:
### Description
When `backend_config` is missing required keys (`backend_name` or `backend_options`), the code raises a generic `KeyError` that doesn't clearly indicate what's missing or what's required. This makes debugging difficult for users who accidentally omit required configuration keys.

The docstring in `QuMat.__init__()` claims it raises `KeyError` for missing keys, but the error message is not helpful. Additionally, there's inconsistency in how backends handle missing keys - some use `.get()` with defaults (Cirq), while others use direct dictionary access (Qiskit, Amazon Braket).

### Expected Behavior

Raises a generic `KeyError`:

issues.title: [Feature] Parameter Optimization Utilities for Parameterized Quantum Circuits
issues.body:
### Summary
Add built-in parameter optimization utilities to QuMat, including the parameter shift rule for gradient computation and optimizer wrappers for training parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs). This will eliminate the need for users to write optimization boilerplate code and make QuMat a more complete quantum ML framework.

### Use Case
Users currently write 50+ lines of custom optimization loops. The `docs/qumat_gap_analysis_for_pqc.md` explicitly identifies this as missing:
- Section 4: "No functionality for optimizing parameters"
- Part 2, Section 1: "Parameter Shift Rule Implementation" listed as "Required Additions"
- Part 2, Section 4: "Optimization Module" listed as "Required Additions"
Current: Users write manual scipy.optimize loops with parameter binding
Proposed: `optimize_parameters(qumat, cost_fn, initial_params, method="gradient_descent")`

issues.title: [Docs] Key QuMat methods (swap_test, measure_overlap, U-gate, state vector) missing from API docs
issues.body:
Several useful `QuMat` methods exist in `qumat/qumat.py` but are not mentioned in `docs/api.md`, so users reading the API docs won’t discover them. Examples: `swap_test`, `measure_overlap`, `get_final_state_vector`, `apply_u_gate`, `apply_cswap_gate`.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Open `docs/api.md` and look at the list of documented `QuMat` methods (init, basic gates, rotation gates, `execute_circuit`, `bind_parameters`, `_handle_parameter`, etc.).
2. Open `qumat/qumat.py` and scroll through the `QuMat` class.
3. Notice that methods like `swap_test`, `measure_overlap`, `get_final_state_vector`, `apply_u_gate`, `apply_cswap_gate` are implemented but never mentioned in `docs/api.md`.

### Expected Behavior

The main API docs should list and briefly explain all important public `QuMat` methods, especially higher-level ones like:
- `get_final_state_vector(self)`
- `apply_u_gate(self, qubit_index, theta, phi, lambd)`
- `apply_cswap_gate(self, ...)`
- `swap_test(self, ancilla_qubit, qubit1, qubit2)`
- `measure_overlap(self, qubit1, qubit2, ancilla_qubit=0)`

### Actual Behavior

These methods are available in code (and used in tests), but they are missing from `docs/api.md`, making them easy to miss for users relying on the documentation.

### Screenshots or Logs

Not required; the gap is visible by comparing `docs/api.md` with `qumat/qumat.py`.

### Environment

- **OS:** Any
- **Python Version:** 3.10+
- **Mahout/Qumat Version:** main
- **Files:** `docs/api.md`, `qumat/qumat.py`

### Additional Context

These are some of the most interesting features of Qumat (overlap, swap test, U-gate, state vector), so documenting them would make the library much more discoverable for new users.

issues.title: [Docs] T-gate documented in basic_gates.md but not implemented in Qumat
issues.body:
`docs/basic_gates.md` says T-gate support was "Added" and describes the T-gate, but there is no T-gate method in `qumat/qumat.py` or in any backend (Qiskit/Cirq/Braket). So the docs advertise a gate that doesn’t exist in the API.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open `docs/basic_gates.md` and find the section `## T-Gate (π/8 Gate) (New Addition)` and the bullet `- **Added T-Gate** (New Addition)` under **Updates**.
2. Search the code for a T-gate API, e.g. `grep "apply_t_gate" -R qumat/`.
3. Observe that no T-gate method exists in `QuMat` or the backend modules.

### Expected Behavior

Either:
- There should be a T-gate API (e.g. `apply_t_gate(self, qubit_index)` with backend support), **or**
- The docs should not claim T-gate was “Added” and should clearly say it’s not implemented yet (or remove the section).

### Actual Behavior

The docs describe the T-gate and list it as a new addition, but the public Qumat API and backend code do not provide any T-gate operation.

### Screenshots or Logs

Not strictly needed; the mismatch is visible by comparing `docs/basic_gates.md` and the `qumat/` Python files.

### Environment

- **OS:** Any
- **Python Version:** 3.10+
- **Mahout/Qumat Version:** main
- **Files:** `docs/basic_gates.md`, `qumat/qumat.py`, backends in `qumat/`

issues.title: [Bug]  get_final_state_vector fails with measurements
issues.body:
### Description

The `execute_circuit()` function in `qiskit_backend.py` uses an incorrect check to determine if measurements already exist in the circuit. It checks `circuit.cregs` (classical registers) instead of checking if measurements are actually present. This causes duplicate measurements to be added every time `execute_circuit()` is called multiple times on the same circuit, corrupting the circuit state and producing incorrect results.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a circuit and apply some gates:on
qumat = QuMat(backend_config)
qumat.create_empty_circuit(2)
qumat.apply_hadamard_gate(0)
qumat.apply_cnot_gate(0, 1)
2. Execute the circuit multiple times:
results1 = qumat.execute_circuit()  # First execution - adds measurements
results2 = qumat.execute_circuit()  # Second execution - ADDS DUPLICATE MEASUREMENTS!3. Observe that the circuit now has duplicate measurement operations

### Expected Behavior

The circuit should check if measurements already exist using the proper Qiskit API (e.g., checking circuit data for measurement operations) and only add measurements once, even if `execute_circuit()` is called multiple times.

### Actual Behavior

Current code in `qumat/qiskit_backend.py` lines 90-91:
if not circuit.cregs:
    circuit.measure_all()This checks for classical registers (`cregs`), not measurements. Since `measure_all()` automatically creates classical registers, the check `not circuit.cregs` will always be True on the first call, but may incorrectly evaluate on subsequent calls, leading to duplicate measurements.

issues.title: [Bug] No qubit index validation  allows out of bounds access causing cryptic backend errors
issues.body:
### Description
None of the gate application methods in `qumat.py` validate that qubit indices are within valid bounds (0 to num_qubits-1). This allows users to apply gates to non-existent qubits, causing cryptic backend-specific errors instead of clear validation messages at the frontend layer.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Create a 2-qubit circuit: `qumat.create_empty_circuit(2)`
2. Try to apply a gate to qubit index 5 (out of bounds): `qumat.apply_hadamard_gate(5)`
3. Try to apply a gate to negative index: `qumat.apply_cnot_gate(-1, 0)`
4. Observe backend-specific cryptic errors instead of clear validation

### Expected Behavior
Qumat should validate qubit indices at the frontend layer and raise clear `ValueError` or `IndexError` with descriptive messages like:
ValueError: qubit index 5 out of range for circuit with 2 qubits (valid range: 0-1)


### Actual Behavior
**Current code** (example from `apply_hadamard_gate` in `qumat/qumat.py` lines 94-105):
def apply_hadamard_gate(self, qubit_index):
    """Apply a Hadamard gate to the specified qubit."""
    self._ensure_circuit_initialized()
    self.backend_module.apply_hadamard_gate(self.circuit, qubit_index)
    #No validation that 0 <= qubit_index < self.num_qubits**Backend-specific errors** that result:
- **Qiskit**: `CircuitError: "Index 5 out of range for size 2"`
- **Cirq**: May create new LineQubit silently or crash during execution
- **Braket**: Backend-specific validation errors

issues.title: [Bug] Incorrect U-gate decomposition in Cirq and Amazon Braket backends.
issues.body:
### Description
The U-gate implementation in Cirq and Amazon Braket backends uses incorrect decomposition, producing wrong quantum states and breaking cross-backend consistency. The universal single-qubit U-gate should decompose as U(θ, φ, λ) = Rz(φ) · Ry(θ) · Rz(λ), but both backends currently use incorrect gate sequences.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Create a quantum circuit with 1 qubit using Cirq or Amazon Braket backend
2. Apply U-gate with parameters: `apply_u_gate(0, theta=π/2, phi=π/4, lambd=π/8)`
3. Execute circuit and measure state vector
4. Compare results with Qiskit backend using same parameters
5. Observe different quantum states across backends

### Expected Behavior
All three backends (Qiskit, Cirq, Amazon Braket) should produce identical quantum states for the same U-gate parameters. The correct decomposition is:
U(θ, φ, λ) = Rz(φ) · Ry(θ) · Rz(λ)

### Actual Behavior
Qiskit (CORRECT):Uses native `circuit.u(theta, phi, lambd, qubit_index)` - produces correct states

issues.title: [Bug] Cirq backend crashes when adding measurements to 0-qubit circuits
issues.body:
### Description

The Cirq backend crashes when attempting to add measurements to a circuit with zero qubits. The `execute_circuit` function in `cirq_backend.py` calls `cirq.measure(*circuit.all_qubits())` on empty circuits, which raises an error because you cannot measure an empty list of qubits.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a QuMat instance with Cirq backend
2. Create an empty circuit with 0 qubits
3. Call `execute_circuit()` directly on the backend (bypassing the main QuMat wrapper)

**Example code:**
from qumat import QuMat

config = {
    "backend_name": "cirq",
    "backend_options": {"shots": 1024}
}
qumat = QuMat(config)
qumat.create_empty_circuit(0)


### Actual Behavior

Crashes with backend-specific error when attempting to call `cirq.measure(*[])` on an empty qubit list. The error message is cryptic and doesn't explain the root cause (0 qubits).

issues.title: [Bug] bind_parameters silently ignores unknown parameters causing cryptic errors
issues.body:
### Description

The `bind_parameters` method silently ignores parameter names that don't exist in the circuit. When users pass incorrect parameter names (typos, wrong names, etc.), no error is raised. This causes the parameter to remain unbound (`None`), leading to cryptic errors later during circuit execution deep in backend code.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a parameterized quantum circuit and add a parameterized gate
2. Call `execute_circuit()` with an incorrect parameter name (typo or wrong name)
3. Observe that no error is raised during parameter binding
4. Circuit execution fails with cryptic error message

**Example code:**
```python
from qumat import QuMat

config = {
    "backend_name": "qiskit",
    "backend_options": {"simulator_type": "aer_simulator", "shots": 1024}
}
qumat = QuMat(config)
qumat.create_empty_circuit(1)
qumat.apply_rx_gate(0, "theta")

# Execute with TYPO: "thta" instead of "theta"
result = qumat.execute_circuit({"thta": 3.14})
```

### Expected Behavior

Should raise an immediate, clear error message:

### What is the problem you're trying to solve

I’ve noticed that our current OpenSF coverage is at 18%, which is well below what comparable open-source CNCF projects usually aim for (many are in the 30–50%+ range)
Why this matters:
	•	Improved out-of-the-box experience: Users benefit from more complete workflows and less trial-and-error.
	•	Stronger contributor morale: Higher coverage signals a high standard for quality and documentation—motivating new contributors.
	•	Elevated project reputation: Demonstrates professional-level readiness, especially for workloads like AI/ML/HPC.




### Describe the solution you'd like

What I’d like to propose:

	2.	Identify gaps—highlight key untested modules and prioritize writing tests.
	3.	Track with CI—add coverage badge to the README and fail builds if coverage drops.
